findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE`, `_assignee_matches`, and `_extract_jira_field` (imported alongside `_map_local_to_jira_fields`/`_diff_fields` at `outbound_differ.py:63`) are in scope for the neutrality-gate rewire, and if excluded, state the rationale (e.g., they are re-exported for test-suite compatibility via `noqa: F401` and will be handled separately).
evidence: Plan states: 'outbound_differ.py:63 — from rebar_reconciler.outbound_fields import _map_local_to_jira_fields, _diff_fields'

Actual outbound_differ.py:63-69: 'from rebar_reconciler.outbound_fields import (
    _LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE,
    _assignee_matches,
    _diff_fields,
    _extract_jira_field,
    _map_local_to_jira_fields,
)'
_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE, _assignee_matches, _extract_jira_field are also vendor field-mapping symbols from outbound_fields in a core module
AC1 neutrality grep covers 'root vendor modules (outbound_fields/…)' — these imports would be flagged
findings.finding: The plan's 'Confirmed leak sites' entry for outbound_differ.py:63 names only `_map_local_to_jira_fields` and `_diff_fields` as the imported vendor symbols, but the actual import block at that line also imports `_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE`, `_assignee_matches`, and `_extract_jira_field` from `rebar_reconciler.outbound_fields`. These three additional symbols are also vendor-specific outbound_fields names in a core module. The plan provides no rationale for excluding them from the rewire scope, yet AC1's neutrality grep (`none of the 12 rewired core modules import … root vendor modules`) would catch them — so either they are implicitly in scope (and the task is underspecified) or they are intentionally excluded (and the plan needs a stated rationale).
scenarios: If implementor reads the plan's enumeration as exhaustive and only routes the two named symbols, the neutrality grep in AC1 still fails because _LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE/_assignee_matches/_extract_jira_field remain as direct outbound_fields imports in outbound_differ.py.
If these three are intentionally excluded (e.g. they are re-exported for test compatibility per the noqa:F401 comment), the plan should say so explicitly to avoid implementor confusion.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical in the outbound_differ.py:63 leak-site entry: '— `from rebar_reconciler.outbound_fields import _map_local_to_jira_fields, _diff_fields` (plus `_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE`, `_assignee_matches`, `_extract_jira_field` re-exported for tests via `noqa: F401` — these are also vendor symbols; route all five through the Backend mapper or document the intentional exclusion with a rationale).'

make test would go RED on this existing test unless it is updated

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `TicketTransport` (S2) explicitly includes `set_entity_property` and `add_label` (the write methods `recover_pending_bindings` calls on the `outbound_diff_client`), and add them to the S4 AC's transport-surface definition for the `run_differs` seam if absent.
evidence: Plan: 'For run_differs, introduce a new seam … route the outbound-diff client through select_backend(config).transport'
run_differs.py:413: `binding_store.recover_pending_bindings(outbound_diff_client, failure_sink=recovery_failures)`
run_differs.py:392-393 comment: 'Built via the stable acli_subprocess floor … needs search_issues/add_label/set_entity_property'
S2 TicketTransport description (ce4d ticket): '← the create/read/update/transition/comment methods on acli.AcliClient' — set_entity_property is not explicitly named
run_differs.py:416: fail-open except swallows the AttributeError, meaning a missing method silently leaves pending bindings unrecovered
findings.finding: The plan routes `run_differs.py:403`'s `outbound_diff_client` through `select_backend(config).transport`, but that same client is immediately passed to `binding_store.recover_pending_bindings()` at `:413`, which the comment at `:392-393` confirms needs `search_issues`, `add_label`, and `set_entity_property` — write methods that may not be in the `TicketTransport` Protocol as defined in S2 (which focuses on the fetcher read surface for that description). If `set_entity_property` is absent from `TicketTransport`, the recovery path silently fails (the try/except at `:416` is fail-open, swallowing the `AttributeError`).
findings.location: Approach section – run_differs.py:403 site; run_differs.py:392-416 (the recovery path that reuses outbound_diff_client)
scenarios: At runtime, recover_pending_bindings calls client.set_entity_property(...); if TicketTransport lacks that method, an AttributeError is swallowed by the fail-open except and pending bindings are silently never recovered — a behavioral regression with no test signal.

The new test for run_differs patches the new seam but may use a mock that auto-specs the TicketTransport; if set_entity_property is absent from the Protocol the mock won't have it either, and the test would pass while production silently regresses.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section for the run_differs site, enumerate the full method surface the new transport seam must expose: `search_issues` (for recovery's binding lookup) plus `add_label` and `set_entity_property` (for recovery's bind-confirm writes). Add an AC assertion that the new seam test exercises the recovery path, not just the outbound-diff path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan for the `_load_acli` seam contract change — either (a) an expand-contract sequence (add `transport` attribute alongside the old module return, migrate call sites, then remove the module wrapper) that allows revert at any step, or (b) a named git branch/stash strategy and a stated criterion for aborting and reverting the migration.
evidence: 'work one site at a time behind held-out characterization tests' — forward-only language, no revert described
'must land atomically with the rewire' — atomic big-bang, no staged expand-contract
No 'rollback', 'back-out', 'revert', or 'expand-contract' language appears anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The `_load_acli` seam contract change is an atomic breaking swap across ~36+ test files and 12 core modules, but the plan provides no rollback path or expand-contract sequencing — only 'work one site at a time' (forward-only). If the migration is partially applied and something breaks, there is no described back-out mechanism.
findings.location: Approach section — '_load_acli seam' and 'Test-double migration surface'; AC line: '[ ] The existing applier/fetcher _load_acli seams pass with the seam now returning the backend transport directly'
scenarios: Mid-migration CI failure: the seam is partially migrated (some doubles still module-shaped, some transport-shaped), tests fail, and there is no described procedure to revert to a known-good state without manually undoing dozens of file edits.
A regression is discovered after merging: no feature-flag or dual-mode shim exists to temporarily re-enable the old module-return contract while the root cause is investigated.
findings.suggested_fix: Use an expand-contract sequence: Phase 1 — make `_load_acli()` return an object that exposes *both* `.AcliClient(...)` (delegating to the transport) and the transport methods directly; Phase 2 — migrate all call sites to the transport surface; Phase 3 — remove the `.AcliClient` shim. Each phase is independently mergeable and revertible.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Mitigate the `apply_handlers.py` → `acli_subprocess.AssigneeNotFoundError` coupling by either (a) re-exporting `AssigneeNotFoundError` through the `Backend`/`TicketTransport` interface (e.g., as a class attribute or a module-level symbol on the port), or (b) defining it in a shared, backend-neutral exceptions module — so core never imports from an adapter-internal module.
evidence: 'import the class directly — from rebar_reconciler.acli_subprocess import AssigneeNotFoundError (defined at acli_subprocess.py:147, an adapter-internal module)'

The plan's own goal is that 'the core stops importing adapters.jira.* / the root vendor modules' — acli_subprocess is flagged as adapter-internal yet is being imported into core without a mitigating interface boundary.
No mention of re-exporting AssigneeNotFoundError through the Backend port, a shared exceptions module, or any other boundary-respecting mechanism.
findings.finding: apply_handlers.py (a core module) will import AssigneeNotFoundError directly from rebar_reconciler.acli_subprocess, which is described as 'an adapter-internal module' — introducing a new core→adapter-internal coupling that contradicts the story's own goal of eliminating such dependencies, with no interface boundary or re-export path proposed to mitigate it.
findings.location: Approach — 'Exception-class catch site (apply_handlers.py:181)' / AC item 7 / Scope bullet
scenarios: Future stories attempting to add a second backend will find core still coupled to a Jira-adapter-internal module, requiring another cross-cutting fix.

The neutrality grep gate ('none of the 12 rewired core modules import adapters.jira.*') may pass only because acli_subprocess is not under adapters/jira/ — the logical coupling remains even if the grep passes.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `exceptions.py` (or `transport_errors.py`) in the backend-neutral layer that defines or re-exports `AssigneeNotFoundError`; have `acli_subprocess` import from there (or alias to it), and have `apply_handlers.py` import from the neutral location. Alternatively, surface it as `Backend.assignee_not_found_error` (a class attribute on the port) so `apply_handlers.py` catches `backend.assignee_not_found_error` without knowing the adapter internals.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `apply_handlers.py:181` should use the existing `type(exc).__name__ == 'AssigneeNotFoundError'` pattern (as in `outbound_assignee.py:113`) instead of a direct `from rebar_reconciler.acli_subprocess import AssigneeNotFoundError`, to avoid introducing a new core→adapter-internal import that the story's neutrality gate would otherwise flag.
evidence: Plan: 'import the class directly — `from rebar_reconciler.acli_subprocess import AssigneeNotFoundError` (defined at `acli_subprocess.py:147`, an adapter-internal module)'
outbound_assignee.py:113: `if type(exc).__name__ == 'AssigneeNotFoundError':` — catches without importing acli_subprocess
acli_subprocess is described in the plan as 'an adapter-internal module', making a direct import from it a new core→adapter coupling
The story's primary goal is eliminating core imports of adapter internals; the fix introduces a new one at the same layer boundary
findings.finding: The plan's fix for `apply_handlers.py:181`'s `ctx.acli.AssigneeNotFoundError` catch — importing `AssigneeNotFoundError` directly from `rebar_reconciler.acli_subprocess` — introduces a new core→adapter-internal module import, the same class of coupling the story is designed to eliminate. The codebase already implements a zero-import alternative at `outbound_assignee.py:113`: `if type(exc).__name__ == 'AssigneeNotFoundError'` catches the class by name without any import, decoupling the core handler from the adapter's exception hierarchy. The plan does not evaluate this existing pattern.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Exception-class catch site (apply_handlers.py:181)'; AC item 7
scenarios: apply_handlers.py (a core module) gains a direct import of an adapter-internal module, contradicting the story's neutrality gate which asserts core modules must not import adapter internals.
If acli_subprocess is later relocated (S5) the import path in apply_handlers.py will need updating — the name-check alternative is relocation-stable.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `except ctx.acli.AssigneeNotFoundError` with `except Exception as exc: if type(exc).__name__ != 'AssigneeNotFoundError': raise` (mirroring `outbound_assignee.py:113`), or re-export `AssigneeNotFoundError` from a core-layer exceptions module (`_errors.py`) so `apply_handlers.py` imports from core, not from the adapter.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Approach whether `BatchApplyContext.acli` is removed (preferred, since its only use is eliminated) or renamed/repurposed, so the implementer makes a deliberate decision rather than leaving a misleadingly-named `Any`-typed field holding a transport.
evidence: Plan: '(ctx.acli/BatchApplyContext is updated so no other site reads an exception class off the transport.)'
apply_handlers.py:69: `acli: Any` field on BatchApplyContext
apply_handlers.py:181: `except ctx.acli.AssigneeNotFoundError` — the only grep hit for `ctx.acli` in the codebase
ABSENCE: plan does not specify whether BatchApplyContext.acli is removed or repurposed after the fix
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `BatchApplyContext.acli` will hold the bare `TicketTransport` after the seam change (applier.py:656 sets `acli=acli` where `acli = _load_acli()`). The plan fixes the only confirmed usage of `ctx.acli` as an exception-class carrier (`apply_handlers.py:181`), but does not explicitly state whether `BatchApplyContext.acli` field is removed, renamed, or kept with a new meaning. If kept, it now carries a TicketTransport where callers might historically expect a module — any undiscovered `ctx.acli.<attr>` access that wasn't caught by the plan's audit would silently `AttributeError` at runtime.
findings.location: Approach section – 'Exception-class catch site (apply_handlers.py:181)'; apply_handlers.py BatchApplyContext dataclass (line 59-75)
scenarios: A future reader or test adds `ctx.acli.SomeError` access expecting the old module; the field silently holds a TicketTransport and AttributeErrors in production.
An undiscovered test references `ctx.acli` in a way the plan's grep missed (the grep searched source, not generated/dynamic code).
findings.suggested_fix: State explicitly: 'Remove the `acli` field from `BatchApplyContext`; its only consumer (`apply_handlers.py:181`) is replaced by a direct import.' Add `BatchApplyContext.acli` removal to the edit-set and AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Cite where `RemoteRef` and `IdentityConvention` are defined (module path + story), confirm they already exist in S2's Backend port, and add a one-line description of the interface they expose at the `apply_inbound_records.py:70,74` substitution sites — so reviewers can verify the swap is sound without reading S2.
evidence: Plan names `RemoteRef`/`IdentityConvention` six times across Context, Approach, Scope, and AC without ever citing a definition, module path, or story where they are introduced.

AC line 3 asserts the grep will return exactly one remaining `"jira"` line, implying the substitution is mechanical — but the types' interfaces are never verified against what `apply_inbound_records.py` actually needs at those sites.
ABSENCE: no reference to the S2 ticket content confirming these types exist in the Backend port definition.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `RemoteRef`/`IdentityConvention` can replace the two `"jira"` provider-identity string literals at `apply_inbound_records.py:70,74`, but never defines what these types are, where they are declared, or what interface they expose. If they are introduced for the first time in this story (rather than inherited from S2), this is a novel abstraction without justification; if they already exist in S2, the plan should cite the definition site so reviewers can verify the substitution is sound.
findings.location: Context — 'apply_inbound_records.py:70,74' bullet; Scope — 'Replace the apply_inbound_records.py:70,74 "jira" provider literals with RemoteRef/IdentityConvention'; AC line 3
scenarios: If `RemoteRef`/`IdentityConvention` are not yet defined (or not defined in S2 as assumed), the scope item cannot be completed without introducing a new abstraction — which itself needs prior-art review (e.g., a simple `BackendId` enum or string constant might suffice).

If these types exist but their interface doesn't fit the usage at `:70`/`:74` (e.g., they require a context object the call site doesn't have), the substitution silently fails type-checking until `make typecheck` runs.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical to the Context and Scope entries, e.g.: "`RemoteRef` (defined at `rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/backend.py`, introduced in S2) wraps a provider-identity tag; `IdentityConvention` provides the canonical provider string — replacing the bare `"jira"` literals with `IdentityConvention.JIRA` (or equivalent) at `:70,74`."

findings.finding: AC item 3 asserts `apply_inbound_records.py` will have 'no hardcoded `"jira"` provider/creation-channel literal — verified by `grep`'. However, the plan body explicitly keeps `validate_creation_channel("jira")` at `:156` in scope (it is 'a creation-channel vocabulary key … out of scope'). That line contains the literal string `"jira"` and will be matched by any grep for `"jira"` in that file — making the AC's grep gate permanently non-green even after a correct implementation.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria item 3; Context section 'apply_inbound_records.py:156 — validate_creation_channel("jira") — is NOT a provider-identity literal … out of scope'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC-3 verification with: `grep -n '"jira"' src/.../apply_inbound_records.py | grep -v validate_creation_channel` returns zero hits — distinguishing the out-of-scope vocabulary key from the in-scope provider-identity literals.

findings.finding: AC item 3 states the verification grep returns zero hits for hardcoded `"jira"` provider/creation-channel literals, but the plan body explicitly preserves `validate_creation_channel("jira")` at `apply_inbound_records.py:156`. A literal `grep` for `"jira"` in that file will hit line 156, making the AC's 'zero hits' claim impossible to satisfy as written.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, item 3 / Context section (apply_inbound_records.py:156 exclusion)
findings.suggested_fix: Change the AC 3 grep to: `grep -n '"jira"' src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/apply_inbound_records.py | grep -v 'validate_creation_channel'` returning zero hits — this excludes the vocabulary-key literal while still catching any residual provider-identity `"jira"` strings.

findings.finding: AC item 3's grep verification gate states `apply_inbound_records.py` must have 'no hardcoded `"jira"` provider/creation-channel literal — verified by grep', but the plan body explicitly exempts `validate_creation_channel("jira")` at `:156` as a vocabulary key (not a provider literal) that stays unchanged. A grep for `"jira"` in that file will produce a hit at `:156`, causing the AC's stated verification to fail spuriously — or, if the implementor removes `:156` to satisfy the grep gate, they break the reducer vocabulary validation.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria item 3 — 'apply_inbound_records.py uses RemoteRef/IdentityConvention with no hardcoded "jira" provider/creation-channel literal — verified by grep'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the AC3 verification to: `grep -n '"jira"' src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/apply_inbound_records.py | grep -v 'validate_creation_channel'` returns zero hits — making the exemption machine-checkable.

findings.finding: AC item 3 asserts `apply_inbound_records.py` will have 'no hardcoded `"jira"` provider/creation-channel literal — verified by `grep`', but the plan body explicitly keeps `validate_creation_channel("jira")` at `:156` unchanged. A whole-file grep would hit `:156` and report a false failure, sending an implementer to either remove the excluded constant (contradicting Scope/Context) or abandon the grep check.
findings.location: AC item 3 vs. Context ('Excluded: `apply_inbound_records.py:156`') and Scope ('the `:156` `validate_creation_channel("jira")` vocabulary key is out of scope and unchanged')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC item 3 verify clause with: 'verified by `grep -n '"jira"' src/.../apply_inbound_records.py | grep -v validate_creation_channel` returning zero hits' — this excludes the explicitly in-scope `validate_creation_channel("jira")` at `:156` while still catching any inadvertent re-introduction of raw provider literals at `:70`/`:74`.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
